subject: ap-chemistry
title: Ionic Bonding: Polarizability & Melting Points
stimulus:
A student learns that ionic compounds have significant covalent character when a cation has a polarizing effect
on a large anion. As a result, the student hypothesizes that salts composed of small cations and large anions
should have relatively low melting points. The melting points of four ionic compounds are given below:
LiI: 449°C, KI: 686°C, LiF: 845°C, NaF: 993°C

parts.prompt: Select two compounds from the table and explain how the data support the student's hypothesis.
parts.scoring_notes: LiI (449°C) vs. KI (686°C) supports the hypothesis: both have the same large I$^-$ anion, but Li$^+$ is smaller than K$^+$. Smaller Li$^+$ polarizes I$^-$ more strongly, giving more covalent character, which lowers the melting point. Alternatively: LiI (449°C) vs. LiF (845°C) — same Li$^+$ cation, but I$^-$ is larger and more polarizable than F$^-$. Li$^+$ polarizes the larger I$^-$ more → more covalent character → lower melting point.

scoring_points.description: For selecting two compounds that demonstrate the trend: 1 point

required_elements: LiI and KI (same anion, smaller cation in LiI → lower melting point), OR LiI and LiF (same cation, larger anion in LiI → lower melting point).
alternatives.correct_example: LiI (449°C) and KI (686°C): both have the I$^-$ anion, but Li$^+$ is smaller than K$^+$. LiI has the smaller cation and also has the lower melting point, consistent with the hypothesis.

wrong_examples: Selecting LiF and NaF for small-cation/small-anion comparison — F$^-$ is small, so the hypothesis (large anion) doesn't apply to this pair.
common_traps: To test the hypothesis, compare salts that share either the same cation (varying anion size) or same anion (varying cation size).

scoring_points.description: For correct explanation invoking polarization/covalent character: 1 point

required_elements: A small cation (or large anion) leads to greater polarization of the anion.
Greater polarization → more covalent character → weaker effective ionic attraction → lower melting point.
alternatives.correct_example: Li$^+$ is smaller than K$^+$ and has a higher charge density. The small Li$^+$ polarizes the electron cloud of the large I$^-$ anion to a greater extent, giving LiI more covalent character. More covalent character means weaker overall ionic attraction, so less energy is needed to melt the crystal, resulting in the lower melting point of LiI.

wrong_examples: Simply stating LiI has lower melting point without explaining the polarization mechanism.
common_traps: The mechanism is: small/highly charged cation → greater electric field → greater distortion of anion's electron cloud → more covalent character → lower melting point.

parts.prompt:
Identify a compound from the table that can be dissolved in water to produce a basic solution. Write the net
ionic equation for the reaction that occurs to cause the solution to be basic.
parts.scoring_notes: LiF, NaF, or KF (fluoride salts). F$^-$ is the conjugate base of the weak acid HF, so it hydrolyzes in water to produce OH$^-$. Net ionic equation: F$^-$(aq) + H$_2$O(l) ⇌ HF(aq) + OH$^-$(aq). LiI and KI have iodide as the conjugate base of the strong acid HI — I$^-$ does not hydrolyze, so its solutions are neutral. Accept LiF, KI compounds that have a hydrolyzing anion.

scoring_points.description: For identifying LiF (or NaF) as producing a basic solution: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: LiF (or NaF). F$^-$ is the conjugate base of the weak acid HF ($K_a$ = 6.8 × 10$^{-4}$). Because HF is a weak acid, its conjugate base F$^-$ is a relatively strong base that reacts with water to produce OH$^-$, making the solution basic.

wrong_examples: LiI or KI — I$^-$ is the conjugate base of the strong acid HI; strong acid conjugate bases do not hydrolyze.
common_traps: Only the conjugate bases of weak acids hydrolyze to give basic solutions. I$^-$ (from HI, strong acid) does not hydrolyze; F$^-$ (from HF, weak acid) does.

scoring_points.description: For the correct net-ionic equation for hydrolysis of F$^-$: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: F$^-$(aq) + H$_2$O(l) ⇌ HF(aq) + OH$^-$(aq). The F$^-$ ion acts as a Brønsted–Lowry base, accepting a proton from water to produce OH$^-$.

wrong_examples: Writing F$^-$ + H$^+$ → HF — this is the neutralization of F$^-$ by a strong acid, not hydrolysis.
common_traps: Hydrolysis of F$^-$: F$^-$ accepts H$^+$ from water; water donates a proton and becomes OH$^-$.
